title: Justice, Are You Still There?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. This letter's cumulative, prosecutorial style and the sequence of judicial findings it cites are preserved. Court quotations, dates, monetary figures, and intentional English are retained as closely as the source permits.
paragraphs: Speaking at Kallakurichi on the 16th, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, in her characteristic fashion, levelled this charge against us: “When it was part of the Union Government, the DMK took the lead in perpetrating the Himalayan spectrum scam, amounting to two lakh crore rupees and bringing disgrace upon Tamil Nadu itself.” The spectrum case is being heard in the Special Court in Delhi. No judgment has yet been delivered. The trial is still under way. A. Raja and the other accused, unlike Jayalalithaa, have not sought adjournment after adjournment; they have been cooperating properly with the proceedings. Yet at every election campaign meeting she attends, Jayalalithaa deliberately and systematically accuses the Kazhagam of corruption. I therefore consider it fitting to remind everyone of the corruption charges made against Jayalalithaa and her government when she was Chief Minister, of the court proceedings on those charges, and of the judgments delivered by the courts.

Jayalalithaa and Sasikala were partners in Jaya Publications. On 29 May 1992, they purchased 3.0786 acres belonging to the government-owned TANSI Foundry, together with a building measuring 2,698 square metres, at a price below the guideline value. Jayalalithaa herself categorically admitted this at the conference held in Madurai on 30 June 1992, saying there was nothing wrong in making that purchase, and every newspaper reported it. The purchase caused the government a loss of Rs. 3,05,91,429. Irregularities in the sale deed caused a further loss of Rs. 43,09,474. In his final judgment, the judge who heard the case said, “It is determined that the charges levelled against Jayalalithaa have been proved beyond doubt,” and on 9 October 2000 sentenced her to three years' rigorous imprisonment and imposed a fine of Rs. 10,000. On the same day, for the purchase of another TANSI property by Sasi Enterprises, the same judge sentenced her to two years' rigorous imprisonment and imposed a fine of Rs. 5,000.

Likewise, while Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister, permission was granted on 1 November 1991 for the Kodaikanal Pleasant Stay Hotel to be built with a ground floor and one additional floor. When permission for six more floors was sought on 29 January 1992, the Kodaikanal township executive officer rejected it on 7 February 1992 because it violated the Kodaikanal Master Plan rules. The hotel owner appealed to the government. Government officials recorded that this was a violation of the rules and that the petition could be rejected. The file itself bears witness that the Chief Minister issued an order without regard for any of the objections recorded in it. Cyriac, IAS, then secretary of the department, met the minister and pointed out that the order was unlawful. Cyriac was immediately transferred from the post. H. M. Pandey was appointed in his place on 10 May 1994, and the government order was issued on 13 May 1994.

The Palani Hills Conservation Council challenged this in the High Court while the AIADMK was still in office. Besides ordering the demolition of the hotel portions constructed in violation of the rules, the High Court judge said: “The Government had exempted the law-breaker from the operation of the law, which would tantamount to cutting a man to the size of a cot.” It was at the end of that historic judgment that the judge cited the Tirukkural: “The king who does not daily examine and dispense justice will himself decline day by day.”

The judgment was upheld when appealed before the Supreme Court. The Special Court tried the case and, in its judgment delivered on 2 February 2000, said: “It can be seen that all five accused conspired with one another and committed acts of corruption and criminal offences. Disregarding the severe censure expressed by the High Court and the Supreme Court, the public servants accused in this case acted without regard for the public interest. The explanations they offer in denying their offence are like Satan reciting scripture. It has been proved beyond doubt that they conspired, altered records, and engaged in corruption.” Special Court Judge Radhakrishnan sentenced all five, including Jayalalithaa, to one year's rigorous imprisonment and imposed a fine of Rs. 1,000.

In 1995-96, a decision was made to purchase 45,302 colour television sets. At the time, one colour television cost Rs. 12,500 in the open market. The government, however, fixed the price at Rs. 14,500 per set and purchased them, causing the government a loss of more than nine crore rupees. When charges were filed against eleven persons, nine petitioned to be discharged from the case. Ruling on those petitions on 14 May 1998, Judge Radhakrishnan said: “There is sufficient evidence that all the accused in this case participated in the conspiracy. They acted with the intention of unlawfully misappropriating government money. The documents clearly reveal that, through criminal breach of trust, they secured monetary gain for themselves and others. The various documents filed before the court make it plainly and visibly clear that the purchase of colour televisions caused the government a loss of several crores. Though the televisions were available in the open market for Rs. 12,500, Jayalalithaa and six others, while serving as public servants, abused their authority, unlawfully conspired, and purchased 45,302 colour television sets at Rs. 14,500 each, causing the government a loss of nine crore rupees.” It was upon such findings that the case was registered.

Before Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister, her assets as of 1 July 1991 were worth Rs. 2,01,83,957. After she had completed five years as Chief Minister, her assets as of 12 May 1996 were worth Rs. 66,65,20,395. The case was investigated on the basis of a complaint lodged by Dr Subramanian Swamy. Although Jayalalithaa went to the High Court and secured a temporary stay in an attempt to prevent the investigation, the High Court later lifted the stay and permitted the investigation to continue.

Jayalalithaa subsequently sought anticipatory bail from the High Court in this case. Rejecting her plea, the High Court ruled that there was prima facie evidence for the corruption case against her and that it was incorrect to claim the case had been filed out of political hostility.

That ruling was based on evidence compiled from the jewellery, silverware, other properties found during the search of Jayalalithaa's residence, and the deeds for bungalows purchased from Thiruvanmiyur all the way to Mamallapuram.

Similarly, three lakh US dollars were credited to Jayalalithaa's bank account. A case was filed under the foreign-exchange law. Jayalalithaa said she did not even know who had sent the dollars. That case, too, is under way. When she filed a bail petition in it, Justice Sivappa ruled on 6 December 1996: “The argument advanced on Jayalalithaa's behalf that false cases had deliberately been filed against her out of political malice cannot be accepted. There is preliminary evidence for this charge. For the investigating officers, uncovering the alleged corruption is more important than individual liberty. An examination of the prosecution documents indicates that these cases were not filed with an ulterior motive.” That judgment, too, was reported in every newspaper.

Jayalalithaa challenged in the Madras High Court the freezing of Rs. 3.58 crore held in her name at Canara Bank, Mylapore, and at Shriram Finance. Justice Ramamurthi ruled: “The investigation into the case concerning Jayalalithaa's assets is at a crucial stage. Her assertion that the money deposited in the bank in her name was not improperly acquired cannot now be accepted.”

On Jayalalithaa's appeal in the TANSI case, filed on 12 September 1995 while she was still Chief Minister, Supreme Court Justices S. P. Bharucha, Faizan Uddin and S. P. Majmudar observed: “The High Court stated that this appeal would cause delay and that, while the appellant [Jayalalithaa] continued to hold office, no delay whatsoever in this case could be tolerated. There appears to be prima facie evidence in this case to proceed against Jayalalithaa under the relevant provisions of law.”

In its judgment in the TANSI case on 25 November 2003, the Supreme Court said: “Criminal law insists that those in public life maintain at least minimum standards of conduct and morality. Those holding high office must not hide behind that law, but must conduct themselves with the highest integrity. People in public life must respect standards of conduct; they must not hide in the twists and turns of criminal law and escape. There is a growing tendency among holders of high office to enlarge their wealth by using government property, or to convert what belongs to the government into their own. We strongly condemn such a tendency.”
That was the Supreme Court's observation about Jayalalithaa.

Jayalalithaa filed a case in the High Court claiming that the establishment of Special Courts to try cases against her was politically motivated. Madras High Court Chief Justice Liberhan and Justice Padmanabhan, who heard it, ruled: “The establishment of Special Courts to try the corruption that occurred under Jayalalithaa's government is legally valid. There is no political motive in it. The government order issued on 30 April 1997 establishing the Special Courts is not contrary to law. This arrangement was made so that the corruption cases could be heard and completed from day to day. Moreover, since prima facie evidence exists in these cases, the accused must face trial.”

Cases were registered against Jayalalithaa on seven charges, including purchasing government-owned TANSI land while serving as Chief Minister, granting concessions to Meena Advertising, permitting the Pleasant Stay Hotel in Kodaikanal to violate the rules, and corruption in the purchase of television sets. She petitioned the Madras High Court for blanket anticipatory bail to avoid arrest. Rejecting that petition on 11 October 1996, Justice Sivappa ruled: “In the Pleasant Stay Hotel case and the Meena Advertising case, in which the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption has filed documents, it is clearly evident that grounds exist for prosecution. It is clear that the proceedings are not founded on false accusations, as Jayalalithaa claims.”

In a case filed in the High Court by Janata Party leader Subramanian Swamy concerning the sale of SPIC shares, Justice Venkatachalam ruled on 15 December 1997: “When so many government financial institutions were prepared to purchase the shares, there is no answer to the question why the government of the day relinquished them to one private party alone. There was neither a reasonable cause nor a compulsion to surrender to SPIC the right to purchase the shares. Even when ordinary goods worth one hundred rupees are bought or sold, both sides bargain. Yet no attempt was made even to bargain over the sale of TIDCO shares worth crores of rupees. A matter involving such a vast sum should have been decided by convening the Cabinet. The government of the day did not do so. Jayalalithaa misused the authority of government vested in her and acted unlawfully. Her conduct causes anguish to the judiciary. I am compelled to order M. A. Chidambaram, A. C. Muthiah and Jayalalithaa, jointly or individually, to compensate within six months the loss of Rs. 28.29 crore caused to the government.”

Jayalalithaa claimed an income of Rs. 60 lakh from grapes grown and sold at her Hyderabad vineyard between 1987 and 1993. The Income Tax Department pointed out that no grapes had been cultivated there during the period she specified. There is a case about that, too!

Jayalalithaa did not file an income-tax return for 1993-94, thereby concealing an income of one crore rupees. A charge sheet has already been filed against her in that case. It is presently pending before the court.

On 24 February 2006, while Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister, Supreme Court Justices B. N. Agrawal and A. K. Mathur criticised her in this very case: “You (Jayalalithaa) are making a mockery of the Judicial Process. How long you can drag the proceedings?” Jayalalithaa has that distinction to her credit as well!

In the case concerning the burning to death of the women students of the agricultural college in Dharmapuri, High Court Justice Kanakaraj said on 20 September 2003: “Because the accused in this case belonged to the ruling party, the police officers treated the case like an orphaned child. They acted in a manner that undermined public faith and confidence in the police. For this Himalayan blunder, this Court censures the Home Secretary, the Additional Superintendent of Police, CB-CID, and the other responsible officers.”

In one of its judgments concerning the disproportionate-assets case now being heard by the Special Court in Bengaluru, the Supreme Court said: “Seventy-six witnesses have been recalled for examination in this case. Most of them had already been cross-examined. When asked why they had been recalled, the explanation given was that Jayalalithaa's senior counsel had other cases in which he had to appear, and therefore these witnesses were being called back. After Jayalalithaa assumed office as Chief Minister, these witnesses were recalled on some ordinary pretext. The public prosecutor appointed by Jayalalithaa's government raised no objection and consented. This alone clearly shows the extent to which justice has been diverted in this case. It is evident that the new public prosecutor is acting in concert with the accused, the Jayalalithaa group. Consequently, a credible apprehension has arisen in the public mind that justice will not be done. It is certain that justice is being diverted. It is regrettable that the public prosecutor merely stood watching, without objecting to the improper petition Jayalalithaa filed seeking exemption from personal appearance when questioned by the court under Section 313.

“The Supreme Court judges recorded their strong objection to the trial court permitting Jayalalithaa not to appear in person, and said: ‘However great you may be, the law is greater than you. Her petition gives no reasons sufficient to justify an exemption from personal appearance. Put briefly, what occurred was an obstruction placed in the path of justice.’”

At the end of the judgment, the judges said: “In our view, the petitioner has raised many justifiable and reasonable apprehensions of miscarriage of justice and likelihood of bias, which would require our interference.” In other words, the Supreme Court judges themselves said that the petitioner, the Professor, had raised reasonable and meaningful apprehensions that justice was being diverted and administered with bias, necessitating the Court's intervention.

In case after case, High Court and Supreme Court judges have from time to time expressed their views and delivered judgments concerning Jayalalithaa. There were also some instances in which, after judgments of conviction against her during her years in office were appealed, she was acquitted of those convictions. But does Jayalalithaa, accused and convicted by many judges in different courts, travelling from town to town and speaking of a spectrum scam by the DMK, not resemble “Satan reciting scripture”? If she does not stop speaking in this manner, the full particulars of these cases, and everything that occurred during their trials, will be published continuously.

Therefore, Udanpirappē, you have a duty to explain clearly to the people of Tamil Nadu all these criticisms made by judges against Jayalalithaa and to remind them of the record. It is so that you may discharge that duty that I have gathered and given you all these details.
With affection,
M. K.
